pattern: BFS from the starting person finds exactly the set of people at the requested distance; count their watched videos then sort by frequency ascending and alphabetically for ties.
hints: Use BFS to discover all people whose shortest-path distance from 'id' equals 'level' — avoid revisiting people at shorter levels.
Collect every video watched by those level-k people and count occurrences with a frequency map.
Sort the unique videos by (frequency, title) to produce the required order.

edge_inputs.description: level 1 friends watch overlapping videos — frequency sort applies
all level-k people watch the same video — single result
only two people, one is the start, level 1 is the other
tie in frequency — alphabetical order must break it
starting person has no friends at the given level — result is from indirect chain

stress.description: random graph of 30 people each watching 1-5 videos, query at level 2
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    n = max(4, size)
    video_pool = [s for s in string.ascii_uppercase[:10]]
    watched = [[rng.choice(video_pool) for _ in range(rng.randint(1, 5))] for _ in range(n)]
    # build a random connected friend graph (undirected)
    friends = [set() for _ in range(n)]
    for i in range(1, n):
        j = rng.randint(0, i - 1)
        friends[i].add(j)
        friends[j].add(i)
    # add a few extra edges
    for _ in range(n):
        a = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        b = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        if a != b:
            friends[a].add(b)
            friends[b].add(a)
    friends_list = [sorted(f) for f in friends]
    id_ = 0
    level = 2
    return [watched, friends_list, id_, level]
